items.correct: A soft palatal fricative (ich-laut), made at the front of the mouth.
items.why: Students substitute English 'ch'. German 'ch' has two sounds: ICH-laut (soft, front of mouth, after i/e/ä/ö/ü) and ACH-laut (hard, back of throat, after a/o/u). Neither matches English 'church'.

items.q: Where does the verb go after 'weil' (because)?
items.correct: At the end of the clause.
items.misconception: Second position, as in main clauses.
items.why: Students apply V2 everywhere. Subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, wenn, ob, etc.) send the verb to the END: 'Ich bleibe, weil ich müde BIN' (not 'weil ich bin müde').

items.q: When does 'in' take the accusative vs dative?
items.correct: Accusative for movement INTO a place; dative for location IN a place.
items.misconception: It always takes the same case.
items.random_wrong: It depends on the speaker's mood.
items.why: Students don't realise two-way prepositions exist. Wechselpräpositionen change case by meaning: 'Ich gehe in die Stadt' (motion, accusative); 'Ich bin in der Stadt' (location, dative).
It's klein. “groß” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does 'Das Haus ist schön' mean?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The house is beautiful. “The house is ugly” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many adjective-pair opposites are in the walkthrough list?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
